fn1: Satyavati, who was dark in colour.
fn3: Shantanu was Bhishma’s father and also the father of Chitrangada and Vichitravirya. The Kouravas and the Pandavas were descended from Vichitravirya.
fn10: The word in the original is pitamaha, which means paternal grandfather, as well as being a name for Brahma. But it is also generically used for all ancestors.
fn21: Literally, winner of riches and one of Arjuna’s names.
fn25: The forest Khandava. The story about why Agni was allowed to burn Khandava forest will be told later. The ﬁre-god had been suffering from indigestion and this was a cure.
fn30: Ayuta, meaning many in general, and ten thousand in particular.
fn34: This can also speciﬁcally be translated as Himalaya.
fn35: Days of the full moon and days of the new moon.
fn36: That is, is freed from the desire of attaining heaven.
fn45: Maharatha can loosely be translated as great warrior. However, more speciﬁcally, a maharatha was a warrior who could take on 10,000 warriors single-handed.
fn47: Literally, someone who travels through the upper regions.
fn48: The mountain lusted for the river.
fn58: Dharma has four legs in Satya Yuga, three in Treta Yuga, two in Dvapara Yuga and one in Kali Yuga. The word leg (pada) is used metaphorically and the word also means one quarter. Thus, completion has four quarters, which is what one has in Satya Yuga. Therefore, it becomes 75% in Treta Yuga, 50% in Dvapara Yuga and 25% in Kali Yuga.
fn59: The word vyasa means to distribute or divide.
fn60: Yama, the god of death and justice.
fn61: Shakuntika in the original text. Shakuntika also means bird, but given the context, locust or cricket seems more appropriate.
fn63: Sanjaya was a suta, that is, a charioteer-cum-raconteur. He was an aide to Dhritarashtra, who was blind, and Sanjaya has become famous because he was granted divine sight so that he could describe the incidents occurring in the Kurukshetra war to Dhritarashtra from a distance. Sanjaya’s father was Gavalgana.
fn64: Kavacha in the original text. A charm that works like armour is another possible translation.
fn66: Son of Sharadvata, also known as Kripacharya.
fn69: Subala was the king of Gandhara.
fn70: Gandhari, Subala’s daughter and Shakuni’s sister.
fn75: This Shrutasena should not be confused with Janamejaya’s brother.
fn76: The king of Panchala and the father of Dhrishtadyumna and Droupadi.
fn79: This is a reference to Shesha naga.
